title: Real-Time Updates Pattern
summary: Real-time updates need a durable source of truth plus a fanout path that can reconnect safely.
task: Design live event availability updates without making WebSocket delivery the source of truth.
constraints: Delivery can be best effort, but the canonical state must remain queryable
Fanout spikes should not block writes to the core system

blocks.title: Canonical write first
blocks.body: Inventory changes are committed to the database or event log before they are broadcast. The live channel reflects state; it does not define state.

blocks.title: Change stream buffers fanout
blocks.body: A Kafka topic or CDC stream decouples writes from live delivery. If fanout slows down, the core booking path remains protected.

blocks.title: Fanout service owns subscriptions
blocks.body: The fanout layer maps topics to connected clients and can shard by event id. It also controls backpressure and drops non-critical updates when needed.

blocks.title: Client reconnects safely
blocks.body: On reconnect, the client sends its last seen version and refreshes from the read API before applying newer live events.

blocks.title: Snapshot API repairs gaps
blocks.body: A normal read endpoint remains available for missed messages, initial page load, and reconciliation. This keeps live delivery from becoming correctness-critical.

callouts.title: Senior boundary
callouts.body: WebSockets improve freshness, but correctness still comes from durable writes and snapshot reads.

talk_track: For real-time updates, I would avoid making the WebSocket path correctness-critical. The system should commit the canonical inventory change first, then publish it through a stream to fanout services. Clients can receive pushes for low latency, but reconnect with a last-seen version and repair gaps through a normal snapshot API. That gives a responsive product without letting transient connections decide the truth.
